era_context: The early Hellenistic period continues with the same major powers and dynamics as 180–181 BCE. The Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is in a phase of territorial consolidation; Ptolemaic Egypt is weakened by regency struggles; Rome is not yet directly involved in Eastern Mediterranean conflicts but is watching developments closely. The Han Dynasty in China continues its consolidation under Emperor Wen. This year falls within a relatively stable period before the major conflicts of the 170s BCE.

events.title: Seleucid military consolidation in Syria and Phoenicia
events.description: Antiochus III continues military operations to consolidate Seleucid control over southern Syria and Phoenicia. These campaigns are part of a sustained effort to recover territories lost to Ptolemaic Egypt during the Third Syrian War. The operations are generally successful and strengthen Seleucid control over the eastern Mediterranean region, though they do not yet provoke direct Roman intervention.
events.certainty_note: Polybius is the main source, but the specific events of 182 BCE are not clearly distinguished from those of 180–181 BCE. Dating is approximate within ±2–3 years.

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt under regency of Cleopatra I
events.description: Cleopatra I continues as regent for the young Ptolemy VI Philometor. The regency period is marked by internal power struggles and administrative challenges. Egypt's military capacity is weakened by internal divisions, making it vulnerable to Seleucid expansion. The regency will continue until Cleopatra I's death around 176 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The regency of Cleopatra I is well-attested, but the specific events of 182 BCE are not clearly documented. Dating is approximate within ±2 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 182 BCE is not a major turning point in any surviving historical tradition, and specific events of this year are poorly documented. Modern scholarship has questioned whether the Seleucid campaigns of this period were as significant as older historiography suggested. The regency of Cleopatra I is well-attested, but the specific political events of 182 BCE are not clearly distinguished from those of surrounding years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 182 BCE is sparse and relies on sources written decades or centuries after the events. Polybius is the main source for Mediterranean affairs, but he does not provide year-by-year precision for this period. Egyptian papyri provide administrative evidence but are not narrative sources. The year 182 BCE is not a major turning point in any surviving historical tradition, which explains the lack of detailed contemporary documentation. This year is essentially a continuation of the trends visible in 180–181 BCE.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Seleucid consolidation campaign in Syria
Continued regency instability in Egypt contributed to Seleucid aggression
